speaker: interviewee
text: Szprycer. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you see, I came here yesterday, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tuesday or Wednesday. I telephoned the Belgium television because I didn't know how to get-- I didn't get an invitation. So the BRT gave me a press card. I've got a press card, you see? And so they said, you can go with us. I said, I'll fly over and I'm with you. So you know, yesterday I was interviewed by different televisions-- Belgium, Flemish speaking ones, the French-- French even. And they also asked me these questions because the film, I will show you later. And they said, Mr. Szprycer, I want-- and I told them that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, what do you think about this fella, [INAUDIBLE]? And I said it to them in Flemish. I said, if you listen to the program-- I said, this is a lie. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jews who came to Auschwitz-Birkenau or any concentration camp and had a [INAUDIBLE] came in, never took them out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Polish or the political prisoners, whatever the scum of the-- the kapos. They had-- they were-- they-- they had-- they could survive. Nevertheless, there were some Polish who did fight, who got killed to [INAUDIBLE]. But never I hear that any Polish people went into the gas chamber. And they asked me those questions. All this time, and I said, I, today think the liberation of Auschwitz from the Russians is nonsense. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why? Because my family's not here. Millions of my-- my people's families are not here. No liberation. Same that I said to you, it's too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meeting could be-- that meeting what we do, many times when we come with Jewish congresses, with Jewish associations, we go there. And then I said to him one thing. I said, listen to me. And listen now to me. You at have your parents who died in Belgium, and they can visit the grave. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, he said. They can visit every week, when they ever feel like to be near them, once a year, like the Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where have they got a grave? Where can one pray for them? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is our grave and we come here. They are here but they haven't been properly buried. They-- they went through gas chambers. The flames came out. They're in the air here. When they come here, they see us. Don't worry. They're with us. And we came here today because the Polish government, Walesa, as I said, denied us to have some important part of it. So you saw what happened today, didn't you? He said, yes. I said, wasn't it marvelous? That is the real visit. And when I went today, to that visit, I only went there to see the political side of it, which was bluff.

speaker: interviewee
text: The security was standing there. They didn't want to let us come in. I was standing there, listening to Elie Wiesel speak. Marvelous. What I loved was when the-- the old rabbi said Kaddish. Oh, that-- that, I came for. The Jewish flags I photograph, which are very-- the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], that I came for.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was standing there. And suddenly, one of the Polish security men pushed me to the back. He touched me because I was-- so I said, hands off. What are you doing? What are you doing? He said, yes, yes. I said, don't you touch me. Take your hands off. And the second one came to help him. Then, I pushed up my hand and I showed him the number. I said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what would have happened if he wouldn't have given in. I would have run up to, what's it called, to Walesa. But then they-- they gave me. They said to him properly, come. Take it easy. Don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that-- that was-- was the meeting about. We weren't welcomed there. Walesa, did it-- a political-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to be seen, the rest of the world, that they should say he was a good man.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody got invitations. We came on our own. The only thing I did today was I went into the camp for my sister, the Frauenlager. [SIGHS] And I was sitting there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBS] And I was crying for my sister, Gise, because she could have been now here. 70, she would have been. That I came for. I put the big [INAUDIBLE], which was left today, that I photographed. They didn't take that away. [INAUDIBLE] What it said on it, to my family, to my sister, from a Birkenau survivor, child survivor. My number, that was there. That, I came for. I came for-- and you know, when I came, I thought I would have been alone. But I wasn't alone. You were all here. Everybody was here. I didn't know I was going to meet Serge. I don't know I was going to meet [SOBS] all of you. That gave me strength. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [SOBS] I'm happy. I thank God that he gave me that strength to come here and talk 55 years after.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I fought-- he rewarded me. The hotel was full of Yiddish people. Everywhere, full. I ordered yesterday-- I'm always on my own. I ordered a-- a car. How do you call it, with a chauffeur? Taxi?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. How call the big cars in America? Limousine, with a chauffeur. And you know, I don't know. I don't know, I saw-- I told him when he got into the car, follow-- we followed the-- the group from Walesa, with the police. I was in the back. The whole way we could drive. And Herschel Szprycer was sitting there and driving along with them. Passing everywhere, went in everywhere. We came now, back from Birkenau with all these police. I was sitting there, driven by Polish chauffeur, a luxury. I thank God, as well that I was here. But the most strongest thing is that all you-- you youngsters listen to give it further when we are not here anymore. Because the Holocaust survivors you know, we are now 65. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You see, I think it's my own belief-- Serge thinks differently. He's got a right to think that. I think if the last Holocaust survivor dies and is not here anymore, all these people will find the rest in their peace. The six million, they will suddenly come to-- to peace. Because they're not in peace yet. Because then they know, when we are not here anymore, that we have given the message. And the young people have to continue. But then they will find their rest. And I hope that at Birkenau, we're never finished, it will always be kept. Because this is the only place you can have 100 [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You can have 100 museums and Wiesenthal Center. You can have the Washington Museum, but that ground, when you walk on that ground, you walk on your people's ground, on our people's ground, who never came back, who stayed there. At least, you walk a few miles, maybe a day, maybe a few hours, and you feel-- and you fell the suffering, and you feel the shouting. And this is the important thing. And it has been that after 55 years-- no, after 50 years, it happened, and that gave me satisfaction. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't have to thank me. I didn't talk. You know, I didn't talk. It's those six millions who talked. You see, I had 55 years in me, a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] a piece of stone, which didn't open up. I carried that. All my boys, all the Holocaust survivors, they-- we all carry it. Some of them can't even talk now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now that has been opened up. We have given our message. I've given it to you. I've given it to-- and we are loosening up, and it does us good, you know? Because it's a heavy, such a heavy burden we had to carry, all these 50 years in us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know how it happened that I got that strength. I never learned to speak. I don't say that. My six million people, your six million people, your families, your kind, we all-- they say it. They are saying it, and let them rest in peace. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [SOBBING] There's no saying that. [DRINKS] This Kaddish, which was said twice, we sang them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'm happy with the young kids like you, who listen to me. You have to continue that message. Israel also and Israel will. And I'll tell you something else. You know, when Israel was giving the states to them in '48, you have to thank the six million who did that because the world felt the shame. I mean, you were fighting in the war-- '65-- the war, you know, you had those six million helping you. Because each soldier fighting at that moment said, to himself, it's not going to happen to us. And they did that, and get strength from our-- upstairs, these, probably, angels were with them. A small country like this, yes, but you had those. You were a strong army with those six million [INAUDIBLE]. It comes from a Holocaust survivor telling you the story, a little, little boy. Now a big man, at 65, and I still don't know if I'm grown up. I go back to my childhood. I always search it. I search it because half of my body is always there. When I go away, something always draws me. I don't know. And the older I get-- this is the ninth time I come here. It's the ninth now. And we all have to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I tell you another little story? Of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or is it finished?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you what, in Los Angeles I was. And the most beautiful thing which had happened to me. I visit Ernst Sacker, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he's got three sons. I haven't got any children. It happened that I haven't got it. I don't. And I was invited in the restaurant to eat. One son is-- the oldest, Ronnie, when we came out-- because Ernst always said, Hellmuth Szprycer, only remember his name. He always give me bread. And when we came out, the son, Ronnie, 42, he pressed me on his arms. And he said, Hellmuth, I want to thank you, what you did for my father. I said, don't. Otherwise, we wouldn't have been here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what are you talking about? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Ernst says that that bread is worth unpayable millions. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is what gives you, when you're older and you hear these things, a satisfaction in life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have seen, met a family. He visit from Israel, a man. Ernst invited him, and he's got young kids in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, I don't want my kids to go and visit Auschwitz and Birkenau because I don't give the Poles the money which they spend there. I said, what are you talking about? What are you talking about? How can you say a thing like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, when you come to Birkenau, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like I said, you walk the grounds and you're there with your ancestors. For your children, at least you are on that spot where it happened. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], it's very good. But it's not-- I said, yes. The next day,  he came before he left. And he [INAUDIBLE] me. And he said, Szprycer, I will send my kids there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I will send my children there, he says. And I said, definitely you have to. We all have to. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oy. Oy. Aye, yai, yai, yai, yai.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] And now we're going to see photos of the life of Hellmuth Szprycer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is me in Hamburg. I must have been about four to five years old. You can see that little healthy kid there, beautiful looking boy, beautiful looking Jewish boy. Then, I probably never thought this was going to happen to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the year this picture was taken in maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it must have been when I was-- born '29. Must have been '35 or '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was four or five years old, maybe '34 or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as you can see, this is taken in Berlin, Germany. And my grandfather was, at that moment, 70 years of age. So you can calculate 70 years. At 76-- he went to Birkenau when he was 76.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was 13 then. So it must have been eight, nine years old then. So it must have been in '41, '40, '41. Who else is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the picture is my little sister, Lotti, which I told you what happened to her. You can see that she's also very slim there. And then you see on the-- next to her, the beautiful looking girl, Gise. My grandfather, 70 years birthday. And then my-- my-- then, it's me, that little boy there. And then it's Ingrid, my cousin who went in '42 to Auschwitz. The family, beautiful photo. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1940 in Berlin. On the-- this is my grandmother. As you can see, beautiful looking woman. Look at the beautiful eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fanny Littmann, her name, yes. What a character. This was taken, also, in Berlin in 1940, approximately. Lotti Szprycer, my sister. Just before she died, you can see that the girl has suffered. Maybe in one way, I'm happy that she didn't go through the concentration camps, that she was buried. She's got a burial stone in Berlin, which I visit after the war, which I look after. Look at that beautiful girl there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah, this was taken 1942, just before deportation. Gisella Szprycer. She was 17. Look at her nice, beautiful face. She went to Birkenau the second of February directly from Berlin, like my grandparents, who went to Theresienstadt and later on, to Birkenau. She was also gassed. And probably, I saw it in-- in one of these books. When the women transport went into the gas chamber, it must have been in beginning '43, before we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was your older sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was my older sister, 17 years of age. Look at her, beautiful. That's what I prayed for her today. As I told you, my grandparents were also in Birkenau. They died the natural deaths, had all the suffering. But she-- what have they done to her? Or what happened to her? I don't know what happened. That-- that girl must have really-- all by herself, all of these eight, nine months there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was the photo-- exist from the period the end of '45, when the American army officer found me. I found him, and then he took me to-- to put-- he said, you put on the American uniform to go over to England, to see my mother there. And this has been later taken when I arrived in-- in-- in-- in-- in New Haven by the English newspapers, by or

speaker: interviewee
text: So you can see, it's just a picture when I got out. There was-- it was maybe, eight, nine months out of-- behind the camp. You can still see that face, longing, longing to somebody. And what year would it have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near to the end, '45. The end of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Show the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you tell us the date and what it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the 12th of January, 1946. This picture was taken by newspaper when I smuggled myself into England, the next day. As you can see, it is the photo of the American uniform, which you saw already on the other photo. [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS] About the stowaway, that-- that meant that I couldn't wait to come over with the displaced person transport. So I smuggled myself over on the boat from Dieppe to New Haven. And as you can see, I arrived there. And the next day, all the newspapers, not only the all the newspapers, was full of me. And some of them had even said, 'The Wonder Boy.' Because after those-- I was the first one who arrived in England out of a concentration camp, who came there. They didn't even know about anybody. They didn't even hear from Birkenau then.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that is the-- the-- the story. They wanted to keep under story, to make a whole thing out of it. And I didn't want to talk. I didn't want to talk. I kept quiet until now. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is the premiere of in March '94. As you can see, that to the right, Simon Wiesenthal. On-- in the middle, me. And on the left, my also friend, Max-- Max [? Feitens. ?] He was also in the camp, a Belgian man, who I met later. This was the premiere at which the cocktail was given in the Metropol Hotel. And many people were invited. And I-- I was one of the main important person who made the people come to that film.
